editableNewDialogue.text: I’m comparing weekly market roundups. What’s a reasonable checklist to judge if a weekly financial summary is actually high quality?

A solid checklist: (1) clear scope (markets, macro, earnings, credit, commodities) and consistent publishing cadence; (2) transparent sourcing with links to primary data (company filings, central bank releases); (3) separates facts from commentary and flags uncertainty; (4) covers both catalysts and positioning/consensus (what surprised vs what was expected); (5) track record—can you review prior weeks and see whether it caught major developments; (6) avoids sensational headlines and includes context like base effects, seasonality, and revisions.
If a roundup includes earnings, what specific items should it summarize beyond “beats and misses”?

Useful earnings coverage usually includes: revenue vs expectations, EPS quality (one-offs, tax effects), margin drivers, guidance changes, backlog/order trends where relevant, cash flow vs net income, balance sheet signals (debt/refinancing), and management commentary on demand. It also helps to note estimate revisions after the call and whether the result changes the thesis (not just the quarter’s print).
How do professionals avoid being misled by a single metric in those summaries?

They triangulate: pair price action with fundamentals, check multiple timeframes (QoQ and YoY), compare to peers, and look for corroboration in forward indicators (PMIs, credit spreads, guidance, inventory). A common example is treating headline CPI as decisive without checking core, shelter components, and seasonal adjustments—good summaries call that out explicitly.
Can you give an example of a neutral weekly recap structure that would fit on one page?

One-page structure: 
1) Top 5 headlines (one sentence each, with source link).
2) Market performance table (equities, rates, FX, oil, credit spreads) with weekly and YTD.
3) Macro (key data releases + what changed vs consensus).
4) Earnings (notable reports, guidance changes, biggest estimate revisions).
5) Next week calendar (data, central banks, major earnings).
6) Risks & watchlist (2–4 bullets, clearly labeled as scenarios).

As a historical aside not tied to any person: the 2020 oil futures anomaly (WTI going negative) is a classic case many weekly recaps later used to show why market structure and positioning can matter as much as macro headlines.
If you can, keep it focused on Switzerland and Europe—most of my positions are there, so US-only roundups don’t help me much.

Got it—I'll prioritize Switzerland/Europe-focused weekly roundups (or global ones with strong EU/CH coverage), and avoid US-only digests. If you tell me whether you prefer macro (rates, inflation, central banks) or company/earnings-focused, I can narrow it further.
Quick correction: I’m actually fine paying for a good weekly financial roundup—free isn’t a requirement. I only asked for free options last time because I didn’t want to create yet another account.
Got it—so your preference isn’t “free only”; you’re okay with paid sources as long as the weekly roundup is high-quality and worth it.
Exactly. If it’s trusted by finance professionals and saves me time, I’ll pay.
